body:
This apparently came up in a conversation between @glwagner and @navidcy  and was mentioned in https://github.com/CliMA/Oceananigans.jl/pull/4674, so I'm opening this. This PR:

- Modifies the interface for creating OpenBoundaryConditions with "matching schemes", which now are just called schemes
- Removes the "experimental" warning for `PerturbationAdvectionOpenBoundaryCondition` now that it has more rigorous testing and seems to be working properly
- Removes FlatExtrapolation based on the discussion in https://github.com/CliMA/Oceananigans.jl/issues/4703#issuecomment-3177580181
- Uses tuples (instead of arrays) to hold the open boundaries in `model.boundary_fluxes`.
title: (0.98.0) Modify interface for `OpenBoundaryCondition`s with `schemes` and use Tuples in `boundary_mass_fluxes`

body: Recently, I have been trying to adapt zstar to RK3 by making sure that - tracer is conserved locally (an originally constant tracer field remains constant in the absence of tracer forcing) - tracer is conserved globally (the integral tracer content remains constant in the absence of tracer forcing) - baroclinic and barotropic steps yield the same estimate for the free surface This has been proving quite complicated to do, especially with our SKR3 that requires different treatment for each substep making the whole development quite cumbersome. In contrast, in recent NEMO development, they implemented a simpler RK3 formulation that is second-order accurate and consists of 3 separate Euler steps with increasing dt, so that the whole timestep can be summarized very simply with $$y^1 = y^n + \frac{\Delta t}{3}G(y^n)$$ $$y^2 = y^n + \frac{\Delta t}{2}G(y^1)$$ $$y^3 = y^n + \Delta t G(y^2)$$ This is the same RK3 used in https://doi.org/10.1175/MWR-D-13-00068.1 and in https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ocemod.2023.102273 for the evolution of the slow variables. This PR switches to the new RK3 formulation. To check that the results maintain the same quality, I have 3 different test cases in the validation folder: ## One-dimensional tracer advection (checking tracer evolution) a one-dimensional tracer profile advected by a prescribed velocity of `u=1` using WENO 7 https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/467cb854-d795-4ad5-8cc5-7d4643de75ac ## CATKE diffusion (checking implicit diffusion within RK3) single-column CATKE simulation forced by a suite of different forcings. AB2 uses a CATKE formulation that substeps the TKE with a time step of 5 minutes. SRK3 does not substep TKE ## Geostrophic adjustment (checking the barotropic evolution) This is a one-layer model, so the solution is dominated by the barotropic mode. The video shows a visualization of the free surface evolution; both timesteppers should give the same exact answer, given that the case is barotropic and the barotropic timestepper is forward-backward in both cases https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1962e1b4-ee6d-49ff-8e31-69824ade1024 ## Lock-exchange test (check tracer and volume conservation) the output of the `validation/z_star_coordinate/lock_release.jl` script using both SRK3 and AB2. The integral conservation plots track the integral change in volume, buoyancy, and a passive tracer initialized with a value of 1 everywhere in the domain. As a note, it looks like tracer consistency is not perfectly satisfied as the tracer content grows in time in both SRK3 and AB2, I suspect precision errors that propagate. However, the important thing is that the behavior is consistent between AB2 and SRK3 and a better formulation of a split-zstar method will come in a different PR.

labels.description: So things don't blow up and boil the lobsters alive
labels.name: numerics 🧮

title: A new formulation for the split RK3 timestepper

body:
A lot of slowdown in the `fill_halo_regions!` function comes from the fact that every time we need to fill the halos, we are allocating arrays and permuting them to figure out what is the order of execution of the boundary conditions. This procedure is filled with type instability that leads to a lot of allocation, which eventually needs to be cleaned up. 

This information, however, is known at model construction, so it is relatively easy to store this info in the `FieldBoundaryConditions` and use it when we need it.

This PR does exactly this, avoiding the need for `permute_boundary_conditions`

Edit: This PR is actually becoming quite a refactor. The objective of this PR is to have preconfigured kernels stored in the field to be able to fill halo regions, as part of this PR
- the kwarg `fill_boundary_normal_velocities` is changed to `fill_open_bcs`
- tupled fill halo regions is removed (we need to test thoroughly if this leads to slowdown somewhere).
- we add types that define the boundaries to be filled (`WestAndEast`, `West`, `East`, `South`, ...)
- the boundaries are split in case of a `MultiRegionCommunication` in the same direction as another BC, following what we do for distributed BC. This simplifies the code, but requires a double pass for the moment, which might not be optimal. However, `MultiRegion` is not really used much, so optimization has a low priority there (the cubed sphere is not affected).

This should lead to a cleaner and more straightforward halo regions filling algorithm, as well as slimmer code.

labels.description: So we can get the wrong answer even faster
labels.name: performance 🏍️
